title: The nervous system — receptors, neurones and reflexes
examTip: The nervous system is one of the most reliably tested B5 topics. AQA loves asking you to trace the reflex arc in order, explain the synapse mechanism step by step, and distinguish a reflex from a voluntary response. Get those three things fluent and you'll pick up marks across multiple question types.
introTips: You're going to learn how your body detects the world around it and reacts — sometimes so fast you don't even think about it.
There are three types of neurone to know and a very specific order to the reflex arc. AQA will ask you to put these in sequence, so it's worth drilling the order until it's automatic.
The synapse is a favourite exam target — know exactly what happens at one, step by step, and you'll be ready for any question they throw at it.
intro: Right now, without any conscious effort, your nervous system is monitoring your temperature, adjusting your posture, and processing everything you can see. It does all of this because it has a brilliant system: sense organs detect changes, electrical signals travel along neurones, and your body responds. Understanding this pathway — and why some responses bypass your brain entirely — is the kind of biology that earns you marks on almost every B5 paper.

keyPoints.heading: The pathway from stimulus to response
keyPoints.content: Every nervous system response follows the same sequence: stimulus → receptor → sensory neurone → CNS → motor neurone → effector → response. A stimulus is any change in the environment — light, temperature, pressure, sound. A receptor is a specialised cell that detects a specific stimulus (e.g. rods and cones in the eye detect light). The signal travels along a sensory neurone to the CNS (the brain and spinal cord), which processes the information and sends a signal back along a motor neurone to an effector. Effectors are either muscles (which contract to produce movement) or glands (which secrete hormones or other chemicals).
writeBullets: Stimulus → receptor → sensory neurone → CNS → motor neurone → effector → response
Stimulus = any change in the environment (light, heat, pressure, sound)
Receptor = specialised cell that detects a specific stimulus
CNS = brain + spinal cord — processes information and coordinates response
Effectors = muscles (contract) or glands (secrete) — produce the response

keyTerms.def: A change in the environment detected by a receptor
A specialised cell or group of cells that detects a specific stimulus
Central nervous system — the brain and spinal cord; processes signals and coordinates responses
A muscle or gland that carries out a response — muscles contract, glands secrete

keyPoints.memTip: Learn this sequence as a mantra: Stimulus, Receptor, Sensory, CNS, Motor, Effector, Response. Write it ten times as an abbreviation: S-R-S-CNS-M-E-R. Say it out loud. When the exam asks you to 'trace the pathway', this is the answer.

keyPoints.heading: The three types of neurone
keyPoints.content: There are three types of neurone in the nervous system. Sensory neurones carry electrical impulses from receptors to the CNS — they have long axons running from the body to the spinal cord or brain. Relay neurones are found entirely within the CNS (brain and spinal cord) and connect sensory neurones to motor neurones — they process and pass on signals. Motor neurones carry impulses from the CNS to effectors — muscles or glands. All neurones are surrounded by a myelin sheath (in myelinated neurones), a fatty layer that insulates the axon and speeds up the electrical impulse. Between neurones there are gaps called synapses.
writeBullets: Sensory neurone: receptor → CNS (carries impulse towards the brain/spinal cord)
Relay neurone: within the CNS only — connects sensory to motor neurones
Motor neurone: CNS → effector (carries impulse away from CNS)
Myelin sheath: fatty insulating layer around axon — speeds up electrical impulse
Synapse: gap between two neurones where signal is transferred chemically

keyTerms.def: Carries electrical impulses from receptors to the CNS
Found in the CNS; connects sensory neurones to motor neurones
Carries electrical impulses from the CNS to effectors
A fatty insulating layer around a neurone's axon that speeds up transmission of impulses
The gap between two neurones across which a signal is transmitted chemically

keyPoints.memTip: Sensory goes IN to the CNS. Motor goes OUT from the CNS. Relay stays INSIDE the CNS. In, Out, Inside — IOI. The relay is the connector in the middle.

keyPoints.heading: How a synapse works
keyPoints.content: A synapse is a tiny gap between two neurones — impulses cannot jump across it electrically. Instead, the signal is converted into a chemical message. When an electrical impulse reaches the end of a neurone, it triggers the release of neurotransmitters from vesicles into the synaptic cleft. The neurotransmitters diffuse across the gap and bind to receptor proteins on the membrane of the next neurone. This binding triggers a new electrical impulse in the next neurone. After the signal has passed, neurotransmitters are either broken down or reabsorbed so the synapse is ready for the next impulse.
writeBullets: Electrical impulse arrives at end of pre-synaptic neurone
Neurotransmitters released from vesicles into the synaptic cleft
Neurotransmitters diffuse across the gap
Neurotransmitters bind to receptor proteins on the next (post-synaptic) neurone
New electrical impulse triggered in the next neurone
Neurotransmitters broken down or reabsorbed to reset the synapse

keyTerms.def: A chemical released at a synapse that carries a signal across the gap to the next neurone
The tiny gap between two neurones at a synapse
Membrane-bound packages in a neurone that store neurotransmitters ready for release
Proteins on the post-synaptic membrane that neurotransmitters bind to, triggering a new impulse

keyPoints.memTip: Six steps: Impulse arrives → neurotransmitters released → diffuse across → bind to receptors → new impulse triggered → neurotransmitters broken down. Write each step as a single short sentence. The exam almost always wants all six steps — leaving one out loses the mark.

keyPoints.heading: The reflex arc — fast, unconscious, life-saving
keyPoints.content: A reflex is a rapid, automatic response to a stimulus that does not involve conscious thought. The pathway is: receptor → sensory neurone → relay neurone (in spinal cord) → motor neurone → effector. Crucially, in a spinal reflex, the signal goes through the spinal cord, not the brain — so the response happens before you're even aware of it. This is why you pull your hand away from something hot before you feel pain: the reflex response is complete before the signal reaches the brain. Reflexes protect you from damage and are faster than voluntary responses precisely because they bypass the brain's decision-making processes. The brain is informed of what happened (via the spinal cord) but is not involved in triggering the response.
writeBullets: Reflex = rapid, automatic, unconscious response to a stimulus
Reflex arc order: receptor → sensory neurone → relay neurone → motor neurone → effector
Relay neurone is in the spinal cord (not the brain) for spinal reflexes
Signal bypasses the brain — response occurs before conscious awareness
Faster than voluntary responses because no brain processing is needed
Brain is informed afterwards but does not trigger the reflex response

keyTerms.def: A rapid, automatic response to a stimulus that does not involve conscious thought
The pathway of a reflex: receptor → sensory neurone → relay neurone → motor neurone → effector
A deliberate, conscious response coordinated by the brain — slower than a reflex

keyPoints.memTip: Reflex arc = RSRME: Receptor, Sensory, Relay, Motor, Effector. The key exam point is that the relay neurone is in the spinal cord — not the brain. Write: 'In a spinal reflex, the signal bypasses the brain, so the response is faster than a voluntary action.' Memorise that sentence.

0.question: What is the correct order of the pathway from stimulus to response?
options: Stimulus → effector → CNS → receptor → motor neurone → response
Stimulus → receptor → sensory neurone → CNS → motor neurone → effector → response
Stimulus → motor neurone → CNS → sensory neurone → receptor → response
Stimulus → CNS → receptor → sensory neurone → effector → response
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Spot on — that's the complete pathway in the right order. Drill it until you can write it from memory in any exam.
0.wrongFeedback: The correct order is: stimulus → receptor → sensory neurone → CNS → motor neurone → effector → response. Sensory neurones always carry the signal TO the CNS; motor neurones always carry it AWAY. Get those directions fixed and the order becomes logical.

1.question: Which statement correctly describes what happens at a synapse?
options: The electrical impulse jumps directly from one neurone to the next
Neurotransmitters are released, diffuse across the gap, and bind to receptors on the next neurone to trigger a new impulse
The myelin sheath transfers the signal chemically between neurones
Neurotransmitters are produced by the receptor cell and travel back to the sensory neurone
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — that's the full synaptic mechanism. The key point is that the signal changes from electrical to chemical and back to electrical.
1.wrongFeedback: Impulses cannot jump the gap electrically — the synapse works chemically. Neurotransmitters are released from the pre-synaptic neurone, diffuse across the synaptic cleft, and bind to receptors on the next neurone, which triggers a new electrical impulse.

3.question: Why is a reflex response faster than a voluntary response?
options: Reflexes use faster neurones than voluntary responses
Reflexes bypass the brain — the signal travels through the spinal cord and a response is produced without conscious processing
Reflexes only involve one neurone, making the pathway shorter
Voluntary responses require the signal to travel to the effector twice
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly right — bypassing the brain's conscious processing is what makes the reflex so fast. The spinal cord coordinates the response directly.
3.wrongFeedback: The speed advantage of a reflex comes from bypassing the brain. In a spinal reflex, the relay neurone is in the spinal cord — the response happens before the signal even reaches the brain for conscious processing. The neurones themselves are not faster.

commonMistakes: Getting the reflex arc order wrong — the most common error is putting the motor neurone before the relay neurone, or forgetting the relay neurone entirely. Practise writing the order: receptor → sensory → relay → motor → effector.
Saying the brain is involved in triggering a spinal reflex — it isn't. The brain is informed afterwards but the spinal cord coordinates the reflex response directly.
Describing the synapse as electrical — it isn't. The signal is converted from electrical to chemical (neurotransmitters) at the synapse and back to electrical in the next neurone.
Saying neurotransmitters 'travel along' the next neurone — they don't. They diffuse across the synaptic cleft and bind to receptor proteins, which then trigger a new impulse in the next neurone.
Confusing the role of sensory and motor neurones — sensory carries signals TO the CNS, motor carries signals AWAY from the CNS. Mix these up and every trace-the-pathway question goes wrong.
examTips: When asked to 'trace the reflex arc', always include all five components in order: receptor → sensory neurone → relay neurone (in spinal cord) → motor neurone → effector. Naming just two or three loses most of the marks.

When asked to explain the synapse, give all key steps: electrical impulse arrives → neurotransmitters released from vesicles → diffuse across synaptic cleft → bind to receptor proteins → new electrical impulse triggered. Each step is typically worth a separate mark.

Distinguishing reflex from voluntary: the key word is 'brain'. Reflexes bypass the brain (signal through spinal cord, no conscious thought). Voluntary responses are coordinated by the brain and involve conscious decision-making.
AQA sometimes asks why synapses are useful — they allow signals to only travel in one direction (neurotransmitters go one way across the gap) and allow one neurone to connect to many others.
revisionCardBullets: Stimulus → receptor → sensory neurone → CNS → motor neurone → effector → response
Three neurone types: sensory (to CNS), relay (in CNS), motor (from CNS)
Synapse = gap between neurones — signal transmitted chemically, not electrically
Synapse mechanism: impulse arrives → neurotransmitters released → diffuse across → bind to receptors → new impulse
Reflex = rapid, automatic, unconscious response — bypasses the brain
Reflex arc: receptor → sensory neurone → relay neurone (spinal cord) → motor neurone → effector
Reflexes faster than voluntary responses because they don't involve conscious brain processing
Myelin sheath = fatty layer around axon — speeds up electrical impulse
Neurotransmitters broken down/reabsorbed after use to reset the synapse
